title: No Inference Latency Overhead
concept: After training, the low-rank matrices can be merged back into the frozen backbone weights mathematically, resulting in zero added latency at deployment.

exercises.prompt: What does "inference latency" refer to in machine learning?

options.text: The time it takes to train the model on new data
The delay or time it takes for a model to generate an output during use
The storage space the model occupies on a disk

exercises.explain: Inference latency is the time it takes for a model to process your input and give you an answer after it has already been trained.

cards.title: Zero Delay Upgrades
cards.body: When we add new features to AI models, they often slow down. But what if you could upgrade your AI without making it even a millisecond slower? With weight merging, you get the best of both worlds!

cards.title: Zero Latency Merging
cards.body: During training, we keep the original model weights frozen and train separate, tiny adapter matrices. Once training is done, we can mathematically combine (merge) these adapter weights back into the original weights. This leaves us with a single, updated model that runs at full speed with no extra delay!

cards.title: The Permanent Label Maker
cards.body: Imagine adding a temporary sticker label (LoRA) to a library book to update its information. Before giving the book to a reader, you use a special copier that permanently merges the new text directly into the page. The reader gets a clean page with no extra paper to flip through!

cards.title: Speedy Robots in Action
cards.body: A robotics company updates their robot's vision system using LoRA. Instead of running extra math layers on the robot's small computer, they merge the new weights into the main brain. The robot processes video at the exact same high speed as before, but with its new skills active.

cards.title: Do Adapters Slow Things Down?
cards.body: Many think that because LoRA uses extra adapter paths during training, these paths must stay active during use. This is false! Because of simple matrix math, we can permanently bake the adapters directly into the main model, completely removing the extra paths.

cards.title: Training vs. Deployment
cards.body: See how merging paths simplifies the final model structure for fast deployment.

right.text: Adapter weights are baked into the base weights, leaving a single, fast path.

left.text: Base weights are frozen while data passes through both the base model and separate adapter paths.

exercises.prompt: What is the main benefit of merging LoRA weights back into the base model before deployment?

options.text: It keeps inference speed exactly the same as the original model
It reduces the accuracy of the updated model
It makes the model run much slower than before
It requires us to keep training the model forever

exercises.explain: Merging allows us to combine the original weights and the new adapters into one single matrix, which takes no extra time to process.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Merging LoRA weights back into the main model results in zero added computational delay at inference time.
exercises.explain: Because matrix addition allows us to combine the weights beforehand, there is no extra calculations or latency when running the model.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of LoRA deployment.
exercises.sentence: To avoid extra latency at deployment, the adapter weights are mathematically [blank] back into the original frozen weights.
exercises.explain: When we merge the low-rank matrices back into the frozen weights, the extra adapter structures are no longer needed for inference.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI setups shows a model that has successfully avoided inference latency overhead?

options.text: AI Model A: Runs the main model and then passes the output through a separate adapter model.
AI Model B: Math is used to combine the adapter and base weights into a single set of weights before running.
AI Model C: Alternates between running one layer of the main model and one layer of the adapter.

exercises.explain: AI Model B is the correct example because the adapter weights have been merged directly into the base weights, leaving no extra layers to compute at runtime.

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a model using LoRA and want absolute zero latency overhead. How do you prepare the model weights for production?

options.text: Add the product of the low-rank matrices directly to the original model weights
Run the adapter and base model side-by-side during inference
Discard the adapter matrices entirely and keep only the original model
Add a new layer to the model structure that runs after the original layers

exercises.explain: By mathematically adding the product of the adapter matrices (A x B) directly to the base weights, we merge them permanently. This means we only run one combined matrix operation, resulting in exactly zero extra latency at inference time.
